title: Ten Dollars a Minute
summary: An AI assistant that cost eight hundred dollars a week suddenly costs twelve thousand. Nothing in the code changed.

personality.demeanor: precise with numbers, states dollar amounts without inflection
personality.recurring_concern: monthly AI platform budget of $3,500 has been burned through in one week

facts: daily cost for Bedrock was $1,714 yesterday
monthly budget for the entire AI platform is $3,500
four months of budget burned in one week
engineering traced cost to InvokeModel API
token consumption per query is 10x the baseline
new comprehensive-benefits action group went live eight days ago
CFO has flagged the line item
she wants a root cause and a fix commitment by end of day
number of user queries has not changed
look at what happens inside a single query -- how many tokens does the agent consume per request

glossary.Bedrock Agent: An AI assistant built on Amazon Bedrock that can call external tools and APIs to complete tasks. It uses a foundation model to reason through multi-step workflows automatically.
glossary.Action Group: A set of actions (API calls) that a Bedrock Agent can perform. Each action group has a schema defining its parameters and a Lambda function that executes the actual logic.
glossary.Chain-of-Thought Reasoning: The internal step-by-step thinking a Bedrock Agent generates at each orchestration step to decide what to do next. These tokens are invisible to the end user but are billed at the standard output token rate.
glossary.Orchestration Step: One cycle of the agent's reasoning loop: the agent thinks about what to do, calls an action group or knowledge base, observes the result, then decides whether to continue or respond to the user.
glossary.Token: The smallest unit of text that a language model processes. Words are split into one or more tokens. AWS bills Bedrock usage based on the number of input and output tokens consumed.
glossary.Reasoning Token: A token generated during chain-of-thought reasoning inside a Bedrock Agent. These tokens are internal -- the user never sees them -- but they count toward your bill at the output token rate.
glossary.InvokeModel API: The Bedrock API that sends a prompt to a foundation model and returns a response. Each orchestration step in an agent triggers an InvokeModel call, consuming tokens.
glossary.Cost Explorer: An AWS tool that lets you view and analyze your AWS spending over time. You can filter by service, account, or tag to identify cost trends and anomalies.
glossary.CloudWatch Alarm: A rule you configure in Amazon CloudWatch that watches a metric and triggers a notification or action when the metric crosses a threshold you define.
glossary.AWS Budgets: A service that lets you set custom cost and usage budgets for your AWS account. It sends alerts when your actual or forecasted spending exceeds the thresholds you set.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening -- weekly Bedrock cost jumped from $812 to $12,147 with no change in query volume or pricing
narrative_arc.threshold: player enters investigation, Cost Explorer shows vertical step on March 17th, daily cost from $116 to $1,714, something changed eight days ago
narrative_arc.trials: agent traces, token consumption metrics, Lambda invocation counts reviewed -- model pricing unchanged, query volume unchanged, cost increase invisible from outside, lives inside agent's internal reasoning loop
narrative_arc.revelation: comprehensive-benefits action group causes 6-8 sub-steps per query, each generating ~4,000 chain-of-thought reasoning tokens billed at output rate, per-query cost from $0.02 to $0.20, 40% of queries hit this action group
narrative_arc.return: action group refactored to return consolidated data in fewer invocations, CloudWatch alarms set on token consumption, AWS Budgets alerts configured, weekly cost drops to $1,260

components.name: Bedrock Agent (camber-support-agent)
components.role: Orchestrates patient queries by reasoning through which action groups to call and in what order. Uses Claude 3.5 Sonnet as the foundation model.
components.failure_impact: When the agent enters a multi-step reasoning loop, it generates thousands of internal tokens per query. These tokens are invisible to the user but billed at the output rate.

components.name: comprehensive-benefits Action Group
components.role: Exposes granular API endpoints for plan details, copay breakdowns, deductible info, and network providers. Each endpoint returns a narrow slice of data.
components.failure_impact: The granular API design forces the agent to call the action group 5-7 times per comparison query, triggering a full reasoning cycle at each step.

components.name: camber-benefits-handler Lambda
components.role: Executes the comprehensive-benefits action group logic. Calls internal Camber Health APIs for plan data, copay data, and network data.
components.failure_impact: Each invocation succeeds and returns correct data, but the volume of invocations per query drives Lambda costs up alongside the Bedrock token costs.

components.name: CloudWatch
components.role: Collects Bedrock runtime metrics including InputTokenCount, OutputTokenCount, and InvocationLatency. No alarms were configured on these metrics.
components.failure_impact: Without alarms on token consumption or daily cost, the 14.9x cost increase ran for eight days before the weekly cost report surfaced it.

components.name: Cost Explorer
components.role: Provides daily and monthly cost breakdowns by service. The finance lead uses it to review weekly spending.
components.failure_impact: Cost Explorer data has a lag of up to 24 hours. It surfaced the problem only when the weekly report was generated, eight days after deployment.

system_narration.data_flow: User query enters via API Gateway, hits the camber-api-router Lambda, which invokes the Bedrock Agent. The agent reasons about the query, calls action group Lambdas, observes results, and repeats until it has enough data to respond. For comparison queries, the agent calls comprehensive-benefits 5-7 times, generating ~27,800 reasoning tokens per query.
system_narration.what_broke: The comprehensive-benefits action group exposed granular endpoints that forced the agent into a multi-step reasoning loop. Each orchestration step generated ~4,000 chain-of-thought tokens billed at the output rate. No CloudWatch alarms or budget alerts existed to catch the 14.9x cost increase before the weekly report.

hints.hint: cost increase correlates with a specific date -- what changed in the agent configuration around that time?
look at the agent trace for a single query -- how many action group invocations does the agent make per user question?
each action group invocation triggers a full reasoning cycle with chain-of-thought tokens that are billed -- count the total tokens per query
the comprehensive-benefits action group causes 6-8 sub-steps per query, each generating ~4,000 reasoning tokens -- per-query cost went from $0.02 to $0.20

resolution.root_cause: A new 'comprehensive-benefits' action group causes the Bedrock Agent to enter a multi-step reasoning loop, invoking 6-8 action group calls per user query. Each invocation generates ~4,000 chain-of-thought reasoning tokens billed at standard output token rates, increasing per-query cost from $0.02 to $0.20 and weekly cost from $800 to $12,000.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the comprehensive-benefits action group causes multiple reasoning steps per query
Player traces the cost increase to the volume of reasoning/chain-of-thought tokens generated per query
Player proposes reducing the reasoning steps -- refactoring the action group to return consolidated data in fewer invocations, or limiting max iterations
Player recommends setting up CloudWatch alarms on Bedrock token consumption and daily cost thresholds

learning_objectives: How Bedrock Agents bill for internal reasoning tokens in multi-step workflows
The cost multiplier effect of chain-of-thought reasoning in agentic AI architectures
Setting up token-level and cost-level monitoring for generative AI workloads
Designing action groups to minimize unnecessary reasoning iterations
sop_steps: 1. Open Cost Explorer, which is AWS's built-in spending dashboard that shows you where your money is going. Filter it to show only Amazon Bedrock (the AI model hosting service). Find the date when daily cost jumped from ~$116 to ~$1,714.

2. Figure out what changed on that date. Use the Bedrock console commands get_agent and list_agent_action_groups to see what was deployed on March 17. Action groups are sets of tools the AI agent can call -- identify which one was added that day (comprehensive-benefits).

3. Look at what happens inside a single query by reading the agent trace -- a step-by-step log of everything the agent did to answer one question. Count how many times the agent called an action group and how many thinking steps it took.

4. Check the token counts in the invocation logs. Tokens are the small units of text the AI model processes, and you pay per token. Compare the old baseline (~2,800 tokens per query) to the new rate (~30,600 tokens per query). Notice that internal reasoning tokens -- the agent's private thinking, which users never see -- make up ~90% of the increase.
5. Calculate the dollar impact: ~24,000 queries per week hit the new action group, each costing $0.20 instead of $0.02. That is ~$4,800 per week in extra cost.

6. Fix the action group by combining its many small endpoints into one. Instead of forcing the agent to call several narrow endpoints one at a time (which triggers a full thinking cycle each time), create a single compare-plans endpoint that returns all the data at once. This cuts the agent's thinking steps from 6-8 down to 2.
7. Deploy the fix to a staging (test) environment first. Confirm the per-query token count drops from ~30,600 to ~5,200 by reading the agent traces.
8. Deploy to production. Use Cost Explorer to confirm the daily Bedrock cost drops back to a normal range within 24 hours.

9. Set up an early warning system using a CloudWatch alarm -- a rule that watches a metric and pages you when it crosses a limit. Create alarms on the Bedrock token consumption metrics (InputTokenCount and OutputTokenCount) with a threshold of 5,000 tokens per invocation so you catch future spikes immediately.

10. Set up a spending cap alert using AWS Budgets -- a service that tracks your bill and warns you before you overspend. Create a $3,500 monthly budget for Bedrock with alerts at 80% and 100% of that amount, and turn on daily anomaly detection so AWS flags unusual spending automatically.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Prompt template inflation after instruction update
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of the agent looping through too many tool calls, someone edits the system instructions (the behind-the-scenes directions that tell the agent how to behave). The new instructions are much longer, so every single query sends more text to the model. Since you pay per token -- per chunk of text the model reads -- the cost goes up evenly across all queries, not just comparison queries. The agent trace (the step-by-step log) shows fewer thinking steps but more text fed into the model at each step.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Track how many tokens your system instructions consume and treat that count as something you check before each deployment. Set up a CloudWatch alarm (an automated alert rule) on the average InputTokenCount per call so you catch instruction bloat early.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Knowledge base over-retrieval expanding context window
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: A knowledge base is a document store the agent can search for answers. If it pulls back too many document chunks per query, it stuffs the model's context window (the text the model reads before responding) with unnecessary material. The extra cost comes from input tokens (the retrieved documents) rather than reasoning tokens (the agent's internal thinking). The agent trace shows a normal number of steps, but the step where it searches the knowledge base has an unusually large input payload.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Limit how many document chunks the knowledge base returns per query using the Top K setting. Use metadata filters to narrow searches to relevant categories. Monitor the InputTokenCount metric at the knowledge base query step.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Model upgrade silently increases per-token pricing
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Someone switches the AI model to a newer, more capable version (for example, moving from Claude 3 Haiku to Claude 3.5 Sonnet) without realizing the new model costs more per token. The total number of tokens stays the same, but the price per token is higher. In Cost Explorer (the AWS spending dashboard), you see a cost jump that lines up with the model change, not with a new action group deployment.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Lock the agent to a specific model version so upgrades are deliberate. Review Bedrock pricing whenever you change models. Add per-token cost estimates to your deployment checklist so the team sees the dollar impact before approving a switch.

sop_practices: When you build tools (called action groups) for a Bedrock Agent, design them to return complete answers in one call rather than small pieces that force the agent to call the tool over and over. Each extra call triggers a full cycle of internal thinking, and you pay for every token the agent thinks -- even though users never see that thinking.

Before launching any new agent feature, set up CloudWatch alarms -- automated alerts that watch a metric and notify you when it crosses a limit. Track Bedrock token consumption (InputTokenCount and OutputTokenCount) both per individual call and as a daily total, so you catch cost spikes the same day they start.

Set up spending alerts using AWS Budgets, a service that warns you when your bill approaches a limit. Create alerts at 80% and 100% of your monthly Bedrock budget, and turn on daily anomaly detection so AWS automatically flags unusual spending patterns.

Turn on model invocation logging, which saves a detailed record of every AI model call to S3 (AWS's file storage service). Periodically review agent traces -- the step-by-step logs of the agent's thinking -- to catch cases where the agent starts looping through too many steps before those extra costs show up in your monthly bill.
